card.indications: Treatment of hypertension and selected ventricular arrhythmias.
card.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta1-adrenergic receptors at usual doses, lowering heart rate, contractility, renin release, and myocardial oxygen demand; it also has mild intrinsic sympathomimetic activity.
card.dosing: Hypertension commonly begins at 400 mg/day given once daily or in divided doses; some patients begin at 200 mg twice daily. Ventricular arrhythmia dosing is individualized and may require divided doses. Reduce cautiously in significant renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Give by mouth at the same time each day, with or without food. Check pulse and blood pressure before administration according to ordered parameters. Do not stop abruptly.
card.contraindications: Marked bradycardia, second- or third-degree heart block without a pacemaker, cardiogenic shock, overt cardiac failure, or known hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Abrupt withdrawal may worsen angina or precipitate myocardial infarction or arrhythmia. Bradycardia, hypotension, heart block, bronchospasm, worsening heart failure, and masking of hypoglycemia or hyperthyroidism can occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Fatigue, dizziness, headache, nausea, slow pulse, low blood pressure, sleep disturbance, and cold extremities.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe bradycardia, heart block, syncope, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm, and severe hypotension.
card.interactions: Other rate-slowing drugs such as digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, amiodarone, and clonidine increase bradycardia risk. NSAIDs may reduce antihypertensive effect. Insulin and sulfonylureas may have masked hypoglycemia symptoms.
card.monitoring: Monitor apical pulse, blood pressure, ECG when indicated, signs of heart failure, respiratory status, renal function, and glucose in patients with diabetes.
card.patientTeaching: Take consistently and do not stop suddenly. Rise slowly. Report fainting, wheezing, swelling, rapid weight gain, worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, or a very slow pulse.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Follow prescribed heart-healthy and sodium guidance. Avoid excessive alcohol because it can worsen dizziness and hypotension.

card.indications: Empiric therapy for suspected fungal infection in febrile neutropenia; treatment of candidemia and other invasive Candida infections; esophageal candidiasis; and salvage treatment of invasive aspergillosis when other therapy is ineffective or not tolerated.
card.mechanism: Inhibits synthesis of beta-(1,3)-D-glucan, an essential component of the fungal cell wall.
card.dosing: Adults commonly receive a 70 mg IV loading dose followed by 50 mg IV once daily. Some patients require 70 mg daily. Pediatric dosing is based on body-surface area. Moderate hepatic impairment may require a lower maintenance dose.
card.routeAdministration: Reconstitute and dilute exactly as directed. Infuse IV over approximately 1 hour. Do not mix with dextrose-containing diluents and do not co-infuse with incompatible drugs.
card.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to caspofungin or formulation components.
card.warnings: Anaphylaxis, histamine-mediated infusion reactions, severe skin reactions, and hepatic injury may occur. Liver enzyme elevations are more likely with other hepatotoxic drugs.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Fever, chills, headache, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, rash, phlebitis, and elevated liver enzymes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, bronchospasm, severe skin reactions, hepatic injury or failure, and severe infusion reactions.
card.interactions: Cyclosporine may increase liver enzyme elevations. Rifampin and other enzyme inducers may reduce caspofungin exposure and require dose adjustment. Tacrolimus concentrations may decrease.
card.monitoring: Monitor temperature, infection response, cultures, liver function, renal status, CBC, potassium, infusion site, and signs of hypersensitivity during infusion.
card.patientTeaching: Report trouble breathing, facial swelling, hives, severe rash, jaundice, dark urine, persistent nausea, or severe abdominal pain. Complete the full prescribed treatment course.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions because therapy is IV. Maintain adequate nutrition and hydration during infection treatment unless restricted.

card.indications: Relief of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute pain, primary dysmenorrhea, and selected juvenile arthritis indications.
card.mechanism: Selectively inhibits cyclooxygenase-2, reducing prostaglandin production, pain, inflammation, and fever while having less direct platelet inhibition than nonselective NSAIDs.
card.dosing: Dose varies by indication. Osteoarthritis commonly uses 200 mg/day once daily or divided; rheumatoid arthritis commonly uses 100 to 200 mg twice daily. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Higher single doses may be better tolerated with food. Capsules may be opened and sprinkled on applesauce when the product permits.
card.contraindications: Allergy to celecoxib, sulfonamides, aspirin, or other NSAIDs; history of NSAID-induced asthma or urticaria; and perioperative pain in CABG surgery.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for serious cardiovascular thrombotic events and serious gastrointestinal bleeding, ulceration, or perforation. Kidney injury, hypertension, edema, heart failure, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dyspepsia, abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, headache, dizziness, edema, and upper respiratory symptoms.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Myocardial infarction, stroke, GI hemorrhage or perforation, acute kidney injury, heart failure, severe hypertension, liver injury, anaphylaxis, and Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, SSRIs or SNRIs, corticosteroids, alcohol, and other NSAIDs increase bleeding risk. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics may have reduced effect and increased renal risk. Lithium and methotrexate levels may rise.
card.monitoring: Assess pain and function, blood pressure, edema, renal function, liver enzymes, CBC or hemoglobin when prolonged therapy is used, and signs of GI bleeding or cardiovascular events.
card.patientTeaching: Use the lowest prescribed dose. Do not combine with other NSAIDs unless approved. Report chest pain, weakness, black stools, vomiting blood, severe abdominal pain, swelling, reduced urine, rash, or breathing difficulty immediately.
card.nutrition: May take with food to reduce stomach upset. Avoid excessive alcohol. Maintain hydration unless restricted.

dosageForms: Ophthalmic solution or ointment where available

card.indications: Reserved systemic treatment of serious infections when less toxic antibiotics are ineffective or contraindicated; ophthalmic products treat susceptible bacterial eye infections where marketed.
card.mechanism: Binds to the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. It is usually bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal against selected organisms.
card.dosing: Systemic dosing is weight based and divided every 6 hours; dosing must be individualized by infection severity, age, hepatic and renal function, and serum concentrations. Ophthalmic dosing follows product-specific instructions.
card.routeAdministration: Systemic use requires specialist oversight and close blood-count and drug-level monitoring. Administer IV product only after correct reconstitution. For eye products, avoid touching the applicator tip and complete the prescribed course.
card.contraindications: Previous chloramphenicol-associated blood dyscrasia, hypersensitivity, use for minor infections or prophylaxis, and situations where safer effective alternatives are available.
card.warnings: Potentially fatal aplastic anemia can occur unpredictably and may be delayed. Dose-related reversible bone-marrow suppression, gray baby syndrome, optic or peripheral neuropathy, and severe hypersensitivity may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headache, rash, oral or vaginal candidiasis, and local eye irritation with ophthalmic use.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Aplastic anemia, pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, gray baby syndrome, severe infection from marrow suppression, optic neuritis, peripheral neuropathy, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Can inhibit hepatic metabolism and increase concentrations of warfarin, phenytoin, and some diabetes drugs. Other bone-marrow suppressants increase hematologic toxicity. Enzyme inducers may lower chloramphenicol levels.
card.monitoring: Obtain baseline and frequent CBC with differential and reticulocyte count, liver and renal function, serum drug levels when systemic therapy is used, infection response, vision, neurologic status, and signs of superinfection.
card.patientTeaching: Report fever, sore throat, unusual bruising, bleeding, extreme fatigue, pallor, vision changes, numbness, or tingling immediately. Keep every laboratory appointment. Do not use leftover medication.
card.nutrition: May be given with or without food depending on formulation. Maintain adequate nutrition and hydration. Report persistent vomiting or poor intake.

card.indications: Short-term relief of anxiety, management of acute alcohol-withdrawal symptoms, and preoperative anxiety.
card.mechanism: Enhances gamma-aminobutyric acid activity at GABA-A receptors, producing central nervous system inhibition, anxiolysis, sedation, and anticonvulsant effects.
card.dosing: Anxiety dosing is individualized and usually divided through the day. Alcohol-withdrawal regimens use higher initial doses followed by tapering according to symptoms. Older adults and patients with hepatic impairment require lower doses.
card.routeAdministration: Give exactly as prescribed. Use fall precautions and monitor sedation and respirations. Taper gradually after repeated use; do not stop abruptly.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines and acute narrow-angle glaucoma. Use extreme caution with severe respiratory impairment, substance-use disorder, depression, pregnancy, and older adults.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings address profound sedation and respiratory depression with opioids, abuse and addiction, and physical dependence with potentially life-threatening withdrawal. Falls, confusion, paradoxical agitation, and withdrawal seizures may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, impaired coordination, fatigue, confusion, weakness, and dry mouth.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression, coma, severe oversedation, falls and fractures, paradoxical aggression, dependence, misuse, and withdrawal seizures or delirium.
card.interactions: Opioids, alcohol, antihistamines, sleep drugs, antipsychotics, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory risk. CYP inhibitors may increase exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor anxiety or withdrawal symptoms, level of consciousness, respiratory rate, oxygenation, gait, falls, cognition, misuse risk, and withdrawal signs during tapering.
card.patientTeaching: Avoid alcohol, opioids, and unapproved sedating medicines. Do not drive until effects are known. Take only as prescribed, store securely, never share, and never stop suddenly.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid alcohol completely. Maintain hydration and regular meals during alcohol-withdrawal treatment.

card.indications: Adjunct to diet for reduction of elevated LDL cholesterol; relief of itching associated with partial biliary obstruction; and selected off-label uses such as bile-acid diarrhea.
card.mechanism: Binds bile acids in the intestine and prevents reabsorption. The liver converts more cholesterol to bile acids, lowering LDL; binding intestinal bile acids also reduces bile-acid-related itching or diarrhea.
card.dosing: Common adult dosing begins with 4 g once or twice daily and is titrated; usual maintenance is 8 to 16 g/day in divided doses. Maximum dosing depends on the product and indication.
card.routeAdministration: Never take dry powder. Mix thoroughly with water, juice, noncarbonated beverage, soup, or soft food and drink promptly. Give other oral drugs at least 1 hour before or 4 to 6 hours after cholestyramine unless directed otherwise.
card.contraindications: Complete biliary obstruction and hypersensitivity to product components.
card.warnings: Can cause severe constipation, fecal impaction, worsening hemorrhoids, hyperchloremic acidosis, and reduced absorption of medications and fat-soluble vitamins. Long-term use may increase bleeding risk from vitamin K deficiency.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, bloating, gas, nausea, abdominal discomfort, heartburn, and unpleasant taste or texture.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fecal impaction, intestinal obstruction, severe hypertriglyceridemia, metabolic acidosis, vitamin deficiency, and bleeding related to vitamin K deficiency.
card.interactions: Binds many oral drugs, including warfarin, thyroid hormone, digoxin, thiazides, some antibiotics, and fat-soluble vitamins. Separate administration times carefully.
card.monitoring: Monitor lipid panel, triglycerides, bowel pattern, abdominal symptoms, medication timing, INR when applicable, and signs of vitamins A, D, E, or K deficiency with prolonged therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Mix completely and never swallow the powder dry. Increase fluid and fiber unless restricted. Separate other medicines as instructed. Report severe constipation, abdominal pain, black stools, or unusual bleeding.
card.nutrition: Follow the prescribed cholesterol-lowering diet. Maintain adequate fluids and fiber. Long-term therapy may require clinician-directed supplementation of fat-soluble vitamins.

card.indications: Treatment of selected testicular, ovarian, bladder, head and neck, lung, and other cancers as part of protocol-based chemotherapy.
card.mechanism: Forms platinum-DNA cross-links that inhibit DNA replication and transcription, leading to cancer-cell death.
card.dosing: Highly regimen specific and based on body-surface area, diagnosis, kidney function, prior treatment, and cumulative exposure. Doses above 100 mg/m2 per cycle are rarely used. Follow the exact oncology protocol.
card.routeAdministration: Administer only by trained oncology personnel. Provide vigorous pre- and post-hydration and electrolyte replacement as ordered. Premedicate with antiemetics. Use an infusion pump and avoid aluminum-containing needles or equipment.
card.contraindications: Severe renal impairment, significant hearing impairment, severe myelosuppression, dehydration, or hypersensitivity to platinum compounds when risks outweigh benefits.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include severe nephrotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, severe nausea and vomiting, and myelosuppression. Ototoxicity, electrolyte wasting, anaphylaxis, and fatal overdose can occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Severe nausea and vomiting, fatigue, loss of appetite, taste changes, alopecia, anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and electrolyte abnormalities.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, irreversible hearing loss, severe neuropathy, profound myelosuppression, sepsis, anaphylaxis, hypomagnesemia, seizures, and secondary malignancy.
card.interactions: Other nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs such as aminoglycosides, amphotericin B, loop diuretics, and contrast media increase toxicity. Live vaccines should generally be avoided during immunosuppressive therapy.
card.monitoring: Before each cycle monitor CBC, creatinine, BUN, creatinine clearance, magnesium, potassium, calcium, liver function, hearing, neurologic status, urine output, hydration, and cumulative dose.
card.patientTeaching: Report reduced urine, swelling, ringing in the ears, hearing change, numbness, weakness, fever, bleeding, persistent vomiting, or inability to drink. Use effective contraception and follow oncology safety instructions.
card.nutrition: Aggressive hydration is central unless contraindicated. Use prescribed antiemetics and small frequent meals. Monitor weight and intake; dietitian support may be needed.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible respiratory, skin, ear, and mycobacterial infections and use in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori.
card.mechanism: Binds to the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis.
card.dosing: Common adult immediate-release dosing is 250 to 500 mg every 12 hours. Extended-release products are usually given once daily with food. Pediatric dosing is weight based. Reduce dose in significant renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Immediate-release tablets and suspension may be given with or without food; extended-release tablets must be taken with food and swallowed whole. Shake suspension well and measure accurately.
card.contraindications: Macrolide hypersensitivity; history of clarithromycin-associated cholestatic jaundice or liver dysfunction; and use with certain drugs such as cisapride, pimozide, ergot alkaloids, lovastatin, or simvastatin.
card.warnings: QT prolongation and fatal arrhythmias, severe hepatotoxicity, C. difficile diarrhea, severe skin reactions, myasthenia gravis worsening, and major drug interactions may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain, vomiting, headache, and abnormal or metallic taste.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, liver failure, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, C. difficile colitis, hearing loss, and severe hypoglycemia with interacting drugs.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A inhibition can raise levels of many drugs, including colchicine, some statins, calcium-channel blockers, benzodiazepines, carbamazepine, and anticoagulants. Review the full medication list.
card.monitoring: Assess infection response, cultures, ECG and electrolytes in at-risk patients, liver and renal function, bowel pattern, hearing symptoms, INR when used with warfarin, and glucose when combined with diabetes drugs.
card.patientTeaching: Finish the full course. Report palpitations, fainting, jaundice, severe diarrhea, rash, facial swelling, or hearing changes. Do not take interacting medicines without checking first.
card.nutrition: Immediate-release forms may be taken with or without food; extended-release tablets require food. Maintain hydration during fever or diarrhea.

card.indications: Healing and maintenance of erosive esophagitis and treatment of heartburn associated with symptomatic nonerosive gastroesophageal reflux disease in patients 12 years and older.
card.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits the gastric proton pump in parietal cells, suppressing the final step of acid secretion. Dual delayed-release granules provide two release phases.
card.dosing: Erosive esophagitis commonly uses 60 mg once daily for up to 8 weeks; maintenance and symptomatic GERD commonly use 30 mg once daily for the prescribed duration.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken without regard to food. Swallow capsules whole or open and sprinkle granules on applesauce without chewing. Granules may be administered through approved oral syringe or feeding-tube methods.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to substituted benzimidazoles or formulation ingredients and concomitant use with rilpivirine-containing products.
card.warnings: Acute interstitial nephritis, C. difficile diarrhea, fractures with long-term high-dose use, severe hypomagnesemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, lupus, fundic gland polyps, and severe skin reactions may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, abdominal pain, nausea, upper respiratory infection, vomiting, and gas.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Interstitial nephritis, severe hypomagnesemia with arrhythmia or seizure, C. difficile colitis, severe skin reactions, lupus, and fracture.
card.interactions: Reduced gastric acidity can alter absorption of drugs such as ketoconazole, iron salts, and some antivirals. May affect methotrexate exposure at high doses. Review warfarin and digoxin monitoring needs.
card.monitoring: Assess reflux symptoms, swallowing difficulty, GI bleeding, magnesium with prolonged therapy or interacting drugs, renal function, vitamin B12 with long-term use, and bone risk.
card.patientTeaching: Take once daily as prescribed and do not chew the granules. Report persistent diarrhea, reduced urine, rash, muscle cramps, palpitations, seizures, black stools, or trouble swallowing.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid individual food triggers for reflux and avoid late large meals when possible.

card.indications: Treatment of pain and inflammation from arthritis and other musculoskeletal conditions; topical forms for localized osteoarthritis pain; ophthalmic forms for selected postoperative inflammation.
card.mechanism: Inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes and reduces prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing pain, inflammation, and fever.
card.dosing: Dosing depends on formulation and indication. Oral products are not automatically interchangeable. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Topical products require site-specific measured dosing.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow delayed- or extended-release products whole. Apply topical gel only to intact skin using the dosing guide; wash hands unless treating the hands. Avoid heat, occlusive dressings, and simultaneous topical products unless directed.
card.contraindications: NSAID hypersensitivity or aspirin-sensitive asthma and perioperative pain in CABG surgery. Avoid topical use on broken skin and ophthalmic use with product hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for cardiovascular thrombotic events and serious GI bleeding, ulceration, or perforation. Hepatotoxicity, kidney injury, hypertension, edema, heart failure, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dyspepsia, nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea, headache, dizziness, edema, and local skin irritation with topical use.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Myocardial infarction, stroke, GI hemorrhage, liver failure, acute kidney injury, heart failure, severe hypertension, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, SSRIs or SNRIs, corticosteroids, alcohol, and other NSAIDs increase bleeding risk. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics increase renal risk. Methotrexate and lithium exposure may rise.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain, blood pressure, edema, renal function, liver enzymes, CBC or hemoglobin with prolonged use, GI bleeding, cardiovascular symptoms, and topical skin reactions.
card.patientTeaching: Do not combine with other NSAIDs unless approved. Report chest pain, black stools, vomiting blood, jaundice, reduced urine, swelling, rash, or breathing difficulty. Use only the measured topical amount.
card.nutrition: Take oral forms with food if stomach upset occurs. Avoid excessive alcohol and maintain hydration unless restricted.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension; parenteral therapy for severe hypertension when rapid blood-pressure reduction is required; and use with nitrates in selected heart-failure patients.
card.mechanism: Directly relaxes arteriolar smooth muscle, reducing systemic vascular resistance and afterload. Reflex tachycardia and fluid retention may follow.
card.dosing: Oral dosing commonly begins low, such as 10 mg four times daily, and is titrated. IV or IM doses are individualized for severe hypertension and repeated according to response and protocol.
card.routeAdministration: Check blood pressure and pulse before and after dosing. Give IV slowly according to product instructions and monitor closely. Oral doses should be given consistently; abrupt unsupervised changes should be avoided.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity and coronary artery disease or mitral-valve rheumatic disease when risks outweigh benefits.
card.warnings: Can cause severe hypotension, reflex tachycardia, angina, myocardial ischemia, fluid retention, peripheral neuropathy, and a dose-related lupus-like syndrome.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, palpitations, tachycardia, flushing, nausea, dizziness, and loss of appetite.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypotension, myocardial ischemia, arrhythmia, lupus-like syndrome, blood dyscrasias, hepatitis, and peripheral neuropathy.
card.interactions: Other antihypertensives and nitrates increase hypotension. NSAIDs may reduce antihypertensive effect. MAO inhibitors may intensify cardiovascular responses.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, ECG when indicated, chest pain, edema, daily weight, renal function, CBC, and antinuclear antibody or lupus symptoms during prolonged high-dose therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Rise slowly. Report chest pain, fainting, rapid heartbeat, swelling, joint pain, fever, rash, or unusual fatigue. Take doses consistently.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food, but use a consistent relationship to meals. Follow prescribed sodium and fluid restrictions. Pyridoxine supplementation may be ordered for neuropathy risk.

card.indications: Adrenal-replacement therapy and treatment of inflammatory, allergic, autoimmune, dermatologic, gastrointestinal, respiratory, and other corticosteroid-responsive disorders.
card.mechanism: Binds glucocorticoid receptors and alters gene transcription, suppressing inflammatory and immune responses while affecting carbohydrate, protein, fat, electrolyte, and water metabolism.
card.dosing: Highly individualized by indication and route. Adrenal replacement often uses divided oral doses with a larger morning dose. Stress dosing may be required during illness or surgery. Use the lowest effective anti-inflammatory dose and taper after prolonged therapy.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses with food or milk. Do not stop prolonged systemic therapy abruptly. IV forms require correct reconstitution and product-specific administration. Apply topical products in a thin layer only to prescribed areas.
card.contraindications: Systemic fungal infection and hypersensitivity; live vaccines are generally contraindicated with immunosuppressive systemic doses. Topical use is contraindicated with untreated infection at the site unless appropriate anti-infective therapy is provided.
card.warnings: Adrenal suppression, infection, hyperglycemia, hypertension, fluid retention, GI bleeding, mood changes, osteoporosis, cataracts, glaucoma, and growth suppression may occur. Abrupt withdrawal can cause adrenal crisis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Increased appetite, indigestion, mood change, insomnia, elevated glucose, fluid retention, acne, and skin thinning with topical use.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Adrenal crisis, severe infection, GI bleeding or perforation, psychosis, severe hyperglycemia, pancreatitis, osteoporosis or fracture, glaucoma, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: NSAIDs increase GI bleeding risk. Diuretics may worsen hypokalemia. Diabetes medications may require adjustment. CYP3A inhibitors may increase steroid exposure. Vaccines may be less effective or unsafe at immunosuppressive doses.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, glucose, electrolytes, weight, edema, infection, mood, GI symptoms, bone risk, eye symptoms, growth in children, and adrenal function when clinically indicated.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly. Carry steroid or medical-alert identification during chronic therapy. Report fever, black stools, severe weakness, vision changes, mood changes, or poor wound healing. Follow stress-dose instructions.
card.nutrition: Take oral doses with food. Follow adequate protein and calcium/vitamin D intake and prescribed sodium, potassium, or carbohydrate guidance. Monitor weight and glucose.

card.indications: Treatment or prevention of susceptible malaria and treatment of rheumatoid arthritis and systemic or discoid lupus erythematosus.
card.mechanism: Raises pH in intracellular compartments and alters antigen processing, toll-like receptor signaling, and inflammatory activity; antimalarial effects interfere with parasite heme handling.
card.dosing: Rheumatic disease dosing commonly uses 200 to 400 mg/day, not exceeding recommended weight-based limits. Malaria regimens are indication and weight specific. Dose adjustment or increased monitoring may be needed in renal or hepatic impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food or milk to reduce GI upset. Swallow tablets whole. Separate from antacids by at least 4 hours.
card.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 4-aminoquinoline compounds. Use extreme caution with preexisting retinal or macular disease and other major risk factors for retinal toxicity.
card.warnings: Irreversible retinal toxicity, cardiomyopathy, QT prolongation, severe hypoglycemia, blood dyscrasias, severe skin reactions, neuromuscular toxicity, and neuropsychiatric reactions including suicidality may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain, headache, dizziness, rash, itching, and blurred vision from accommodation changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Permanent retinopathy, cardiomyopathy, ventricular arrhythmia, severe hypoglycemia, agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, severe skin reactions, myopathy, and suicidal behavior.
card.interactions: Other QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk. Digoxin concentrations may rise. Antidiabetic drugs may increase hypoglycemia. Antacids reduce absorption. Drugs toxic to the retina or heart increase risk.
card.monitoring: Obtain baseline ophthalmologic examination and periodic retinal screening based on dose and risk. Monitor weight-based dose, CBC, liver and renal function, glucose, muscle strength, ECG in at-risk patients, and mood changes.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food or milk. Keep all eye examinations and report visual changes immediately. Report palpitations, fainting, severe weakness, low-glucose symptoms, rash, or suicidal thoughts.
card.nutrition: Take with food or milk. Maintain consistent meals, especially when using diabetes medication, because hypoglycemia can occur.

card.indications: Treatment of moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute painful shoulder conditions, and acute gouty arthritis.
card.mechanism: Inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes and reduces prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing pain, inflammation, and fever.
card.dosing: Dosing varies by condition and formulation. Immediate-release doses are commonly divided two or three times daily; extended-release dosing is less frequent. Acute gout uses short-course higher-frequency dosing. Use the lowest effective dose.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral forms with food, milk, or an antacid. Shake suspension well. Swallow extended-release capsules whole. Do not interchange formulations without dose review.
card.contraindications: NSAID hypersensitivity or aspirin-sensitive asthma; perioperative pain in CABG surgery; and proctitis or recent rectal bleeding for suppositories.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for cardiovascular thrombotic events and serious GI bleeding, ulceration, or perforation. CNS effects, kidney injury, hypertension, heart failure, hepatotoxicity, blood dyscrasias, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, dizziness, dyspepsia, nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, and drowsiness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Myocardial infarction, stroke, GI hemorrhage, acute kidney injury, severe hypertension, heart failure, hepatitis, aseptic meningitis, seizures, and severe skin reactions.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, SSRIs or SNRIs, corticosteroids, alcohol, and other NSAIDs increase bleeding risk. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics increase renal risk. Lithium, digoxin, and methotrexate levels may rise.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain and inflammation, blood pressure, mental status, renal function, liver enzymes, CBC or hemoglobin with prolonged use, edema, GI bleeding, and cardiovascular symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food and avoid other NSAIDs unless approved. Report chest pain, black stools, severe headache, confusion, reduced urine, swelling, rash, or breathing difficulty.
card.nutrition: Take with food or milk. Avoid excessive alcohol. Maintain hydration unless restricted.

dosageForms: 100 mg lyophilized powder for IV infusion

card.indications: Treatment of selected patients with Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, rheumatoid arthritis with methotrexate, ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, and plaque psoriasis.
card.mechanism: Binds soluble and membrane-bound tumor necrosis factor alpha and blocks its inflammatory activity.
card.dosing: Weight-based IV induction and maintenance regimens vary by indication, commonly 3 or 5 mg/kg at weeks 0, 2, and 6 followed by maintenance every 6 to 8 weeks. Dose or interval may be intensified in selected conditions.
card.routeAdministration: Reconstitute and dilute exactly as directed. Infuse IV over at least 2 hours unless a shorter protocol is specifically approved. Use an in-line filter and monitor during and after infusion. Premedication may be ordered.
card.contraindications: Doses above 5 mg/kg in moderate or severe heart failure; previous severe hypersensitivity to infliximab, murine proteins, or formulation ingredients.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings for serious infections, including tuberculosis and invasive fungal infection, and malignancy. Infusion reactions, hepatitis B reactivation, heart-failure worsening, demyelinating disease, cytopenias, liver injury, and lupus-like syndrome may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Upper respiratory infection, headache, abdominal pain, nausea, rash, sinusitis, cough, and infusion-related fever or chills.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Tuberculosis, sepsis, opportunistic infection, lymphoma or other malignancy, anaphylaxis, delayed hypersensitivity, heart failure, severe liver injury, demyelinating disease, and pancytopenia.
card.interactions: Avoid combining with other biologic immunosuppressants such as anakinra or abatacept because serious infection risk increases. Live vaccines should be avoided. Other immunosuppressants increase infection risk.
card.monitoring: Before therapy screen for tuberculosis and hepatitis B and update vaccines. Monitor CBC, liver function, infection, heart-failure symptoms, neurologic changes, malignancy signs, and infusion reactions.
card.patientTeaching: Report fever, cough, night sweats, weight loss, wounds, jaundice, numbness, weakness, shortness of breath, swelling, or severe rash. Avoid live vaccines and keep infection-screening appointments.
card.nutrition: No specific food restriction because therapy is IV. Maintain adequate protein and calories during inflammatory disease and report poor intake, diarrhea, or weight loss.
